did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an explainable deep learning framework based on the EfficientNetV2-M convolutional neural network (CNN) for multi-class skin lesion classification. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic images to diagnose skin cancer types, utilizing techniques such as Test-Time Augmentation (TTA), Grad-CAM++, and Layer-CAM for interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing an explainable deep learning framework using EfficientNetV2-M on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a methodology section detailing data processing and model architecture, an experimental results section with specific performance metrics (96% accuracy, 96.17% precision, etc.), and a comparative analysis. Although it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical results and validation, classifying it as a research paper (specifically a conference paper).

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Multi-Class Skin Lesion Classification Using EfficientNetV2 and Grad-CAM++ Visualization
journal: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Recent Advances in Computing and Systems (REACS)
brief_summary: This study proposes an explainable deep learning framework using the EfficientNetV2-M convolutional neural network for multi-class skin lesion classification. The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset of dermoscopic images, employing extensive data augmentation and Test-Time Augmentation to enhance robustness. Performance was evaluated using weighted average accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, achieving 96% accuracy, 96.17% precision, 95.84% recall, and 95.82% F1-score. Explainability was integrated via Grad-CAM++ and Layer-CAM visualizations to highlight critical dermoscopic regions influencing predictions. The results demonstrate superior classification performance compared to prior works, validating the model's potential for clinical applicability despite challenges with class imbalance.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class classification. It had 65 samples in the test set and achieved the lowest accuracy (64.6%) and F1-score (76.4%), often misclassified as benign keratosis-like lesions.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset. It had 103 samples in the test set and achieved 94.2% accuracy and an F1-score of 92.8%.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset. It had 220 samples in the test set and achieved 95.5% accuracy and an F1-score of 88.6%.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset. It had 23 samples in the test set and achieved 91.3% accuracy and an F1-score of 93.3%.

One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset. It had 223 samples in the test set and achieved 93.7% accuracy and an F1-score of 93.7%. Misclassifications primarily occurred with benign keratosis-like lesions.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset. It was the most prevalent class with 1,341 samples in the test set, achieving 98.1% accuracy and an F1-score of 98.6%.
One of the seven lesion types in the HAM10000 dataset. It had 28 samples in the test set and achieved 96.4% accuracy, 100% precision, and an F1-score of 96.4%.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin lesion classes (actinic keratoses, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, dermatofibroma, melanoma, melanocytic nevi, vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into seven diagnostic categories using EfficientNetV2-M. The model predicts discrete class labels via a softmax output layer (Dense 7 with softmax activation) and evaluates performance using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score for each class.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as the primary dataset for this study. The paper notes it contains 10,015 high-quality, preprocessed images.

use.training: The model was trained on the HAM10000 dataset using transfer learning with ImageNet weights and extensive image augmentations.
use.testing: The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, with specific test set counts reported for each class (e.g., 1,341 for nv, 223 for mel).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) for each of the seven skin lesion classes and identify misclassification patterns.
To handle class imbalance during model training by assigning different weights to different classes.
To assess model calibration and uncertainty, specifically analyzing the confidence levels of correct versus incorrect predictions to identify overconfident misclassifications.
To interpret model decisions and highlight critical dermoscopic regions influencing predictions, particularly for high-confidence misclassified samples.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices.

HAM10000.experiment: EfficientNetV2-M with Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) and Grad-CAM++/Layer-CAM

actinic_keratoses_akiec.misclassification_notes: Primarily misclassified as benign keratosis-like lesions (11 cases) and melanocytic nevi (7 cases)

HAM10000.notes: The model demonstrated robust performance on majority classes (nv) and underrepresented classes (vasc, df) with high AUC scores. Actinic keratoses (akiec) presented the most significant challenge due to visual similarity with other classes. High-confidence misclassifications were observed for akiec and melanoma, analyzed via Grad-CAM++ visualizations.

architecture_details.backbone: EfficientNetV2-M (Convolutional Neural Network with compound scaling efficiency)
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hierarchical feature representations extracted via fθ(x)
architecture_details.attention_mechanisms: Integrated attention mechanisms to refine feature extraction

training_details.loss_function: Weighted categorical cross-entropy (L = - Σ wc yc log(pc))

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used during training to mimic different orientations of lesions in clinical settings and prevent overfitting by ensuring the model learns that lesion orientation does not determine class.
Applied with a range of ±20 degrees during training to handle slight imaging angle variations while limiting distortion of critical diagnostic features like asymmetry and border irregularities.
Used during training to account for color variations in imaging, including different skin tones, lighting conditions across clinics, and varying camera sensor color responses.
Used during training to simulate slight perspective changes, preserving lesion structure while adding viewpoint variation.
Used during training to vary the lesion-to-image ratio, teaching the model to focus on the lesion rather than the background.
Applied with a kernel size of 3 during training to simulate imperfect focus, forcing the model to learn robust features beyond sharp edges while preserving diagnostic structures.
Used during inference/testing with horizontal and vertical flips to enhance model robustness and generalization.

conclusions: The proposed explainable deep learning framework using EfficientNetV2-M achieved superior classification performance on the HAM10000 dataset, with a weighted average accuracy of 96%, precision of 96.17%, recall of 95.84%, and F1-score of 95.82%.
Test-Time Augmentation (TTA) using horizontal and vertical flips enhanced model robustness and generalization capabilities.
A modified classifier head incorporating dropout and batch normalization improved regularization and helped mitigate overfitting.

The model demonstrated strong generalization across both majority classes (e.g., melanocytic nevi with 98.6% F1-score) and underrepresented minority classes (e.g., vascular lesions with 100% precision and 96.4% recall).

Actinic keratoses (akiec) remained the most challenging class to classify, exhibiting the lowest accuracy (64.6%) and F1-score (76.4%) due to visual similarity with benign keratosis-like lesions and melanoma.

Grad-CAM++ and Layer-CAM visualizations successfully highlighted critical dermoscopic regions, providing interpretability and revealing that high-confidence misclassifications often resulted from overlapping visual features (e.g., texture homogeneity in melanoma vs. benign lesions).
The study validates the potential of the proposed framework for clinical applicability in automated skin lesion diagnosis by addressing challenges of model interpretability and optimization.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance (e.g., melanocytic nevi comprising ~60% of samples) and potential source bias, limiting external validation and generalizability to diverse clinical populations.

Actinic keratoses (akiec), a pre-malignant condition, exhibited the lowest accuracy (64.6%) and F1-score (76.4%), with frequent misclassification as benign lesions, posing a risk of delayed intervention.
Melanoma (mel) had 12 false negatives misclassified as benign keratosis-like lesions (bkl), raising serious clinical reliability concerns given the aggressive nature of melanoma.

The model demonstrated 'overconfident' misclassifications for actinic keratoses and melanoma (confidence > 0.9), indicating poor calibration and miscalibration due to visual similarity with other classes.

Grad-CAM++ visualizations revealed that the model often relied on texture homogeneity and uniformly pigmented regions rather than critical malignant features like asymmetry and irregular borders, suggesting flawed feature learning for malignancy detection.
Performance on minority classes (vascular lesions, dermatofibroma) is statistically unreliable due to very small test set sizes (28 and 23 samples, respectively), despite high reported accuracy.
The paper lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the claim of 'superior' performance compared to prior works.

No external validation was performed; the model was trained and evaluated on the same dataset (HAM10000), increasing the risk of overfitting and limiting evidence of real-world clinical applicability.
The study acknowledges class imbalance as a limitation but does not fully resolve it, noting that future work will require hybrid resampling and generative methods.
